docs.text: Course Description: Engineering Practicum IV Honors Course Overview Engineering Practicum IV Honors enables students to examine technology and engineering fundamentals related to solving real-world problems. Students examine ethics and intellectual property and design a practicum project — a culmination of knowledge and skill gained in previous engineering courses. In addition, students continue to investigate engineering specialty fields and related careers to determine whether they are good candidates for postsecondary educational opportunities in engineering. This course serves as the capstone experience of the high school engineering sequence, integrating technical, professional, and interpersonal skills developed throughout prior coursework. Students work independently and collaboratively to propose, design, execute, document, and present an original engineering solution to a real-world challenge. Target Audience High school students (Honors level) Prerequisites Engineering II with instructor permission, or Engineering III Main Topics Covered Personal and Professional Qualities — creativity, critical thinking, initiative, integrity, and work ethic Interpersonal and Team Skills — conflict resolution, communication, diversity, customer service, and collaboration Professional Competencies — career management, information literacy, information security, current IT systems, mathematical applications, workplace safety, and professionalism Industry Examination — planning, management, financial responsibility, technical production, technology principles, labor issues, community issues, and health/safety/environmental concerns Student Organization and Civic Engagement — purposes of student organizations, leadership, membership responsibilities, and internet safety Work-Based Learning (WBL) — types of WBL, career exploration, and reflective practice Employability Skills — career research, educational pathways, portfolio development, internship/job application processes, engineering design vs. scientific method Intellectual Property and Ethics — engineering ethics, intellectual property rights, and professional vs. personal ethics Practicum Project Design and Execution — systems thinking, engineering design process, proposal development, peer review, project management, scheduling, budgeting, cost estimation, modeling and simulation, risk analysis, and stakeholder communication Career Preparation — postsecondary research, certifications and licensure, mock interviews, and technical life skills Topics NOT Covered Advanced college-level engineering mathematics (calculus, differential equations) Discipline-specific engineering coursework (e.g., civil, electrical, mechanical in depth) Manufacturing or fabrication at an industrial scale Post-secondary coursework content (university-level engineering theory) Learning Outcomes After this course, students will be able to: Remember Retrieve, recognize, and recall relevant knowledge Identify types of intellectual property rights and how they are protected and enforced Identify the need or opportunity for an engineering solution Identify educational requirements for a chosen engineering field Identify the process of applying for an engineering internship or job Identify the purposes and goals of a student organization Identify the types of work-based learning (WBL) opportunities available Identify Internet safety issues and procedures for complying with acceptable use standards Research postsecondary education opportunities related to engineering Research cost-estimation tools and methods used in the engineering field Maintain a working knowledge of current information-technology (IT) systems Understand Construct meaning from instructional messages Explain how ethical behavior of engineers is essential to the betterment of society Explain the benefits and responsibilities of membership in student and professional/civic organizations Demonstrate an understanding of information security Demonstrate big-picture thinking within an engineering or organizational context Examine principles of technology that underlie an industry or organization Examine aspects of planning within an industry or organization Examine aspects of financial responsibility within an industry or organization Examine labor issues related to an industry or organization Examine community issues related to an industry or organization Examine health, safety, and environmental issues related to an industry or organization Apply Carry out or use procedures in given situations Apply mathematical skills to job-specific engineering tasks Apply model and simulation techniques to create a working model Use the engineering design process to complete the practicum project Use engineering project management tools throughout the practicum Develop a schedule using industry-standard applications to ensure project completion Develop a budget for the practicum project Create a work schedule aligned with project milestones Demonstrate proficiency with technologies, tools, and machines relevant to the chosen engineering field Demonstrate workplace safety practices appropriate to the engineering environment Participate in a work-based learning experience when appropriate Analyze Break material into parts and determine relationships Analyze the similarities and differences between the engineering design process and the scientific method Analyze the potential risk list for the practicum project Examine technical and production skills required of workers within an industry Examine aspects of management within an industry or organization Compare requirements for select professional engineering certifications and licensure Compare professional and personal ethics in the context of engineering practice Demonstrate information-literacy skills by critically evaluating engineering and career resources Reflect on lessons learned during the work-based learning experience Explore career opportunities related to the WBL experience Research a principal field of interest in engineering and its scope Evaluate Make judgments based on criteria and standards Evaluate the safety of engineering designs against established standards Peer review all project proposals using structured criteria Demonstrate critical thinking and problem solving in evaluating engineering challenges Demonstrate conflict-resolution skills when navigating team disagreements Demonstrate customer service skills in communicating with project stakeholders Demonstrate career- and life-management skills by assessing personal strengths against engineering pathways Demonstrate continuous learning and adaptability when faced with project setbacks Manage time and resources effectively throughout the practicum timeline Demonstrate respect for diversity in team and professional settings Demonstrate integrity and professionalism in all project and workplace interactions Create Put elements together to form coherent wholes; includes capstone projects Design and complete an original engineering practicum project using the full engineering design process Use systems thinking to develop a comprehensive engineering solution Present a proposal to an engineering challenge, incorporating research, design, and feasibility analysis Document the full progression of the engineering practicum in a formal technical report Communicate the final solution to stakeholders through a professional presentation Build a complete work portfolio demonstrating skills and accomplishments across the engineering sequence Demonstrate leadership skills through participation in student organization activities such as meetings, programs, and projects Demonstrate creativity and innovation in developing novel engineering solutions Demonstrate initiative and self-direction throughout the independent practicum project Exhibit technical and life skills necessary for success in the engineering field
docs.title: Course Description
Course Description: Engineering Practicum IV Honors

docs.text: Engineering Practicum IV Honors enables students to examine technology and engineering fundamentals related to solving real-world problems. Students examine ethics and intellectual property and design a practicum project — a culmination of knowledge and skill gained in previous engineering courses. In addition, students continue to investigate engineering specialty fields and related careers to determine whether they are good candidates for postsecondary educational opportunities in engineering. This course serves as the capstone experience of the high school engineering sequence, integrating technical, professional, and interpersonal skills developed throughout prior coursework. Students work independently and collaboratively to propose, design, execute, document, and present an original engineering solution to a real-world challenge.

docs.text: Engineering II with instructor permission, or Engineering III

docs.text: Chapters This textbook is organized into 8 chapters covering 85 concepts, aligned to the assignments in Engineering Practicum IV Honors. Chapter Overview Engineering Field Exploration - Research engineering fields, educational pathways, and professional credentials. Engineering Foundations and Industry Examination - Explore how engineers think and how engineering organizations operate. Portfolio and Employment Preparation - Build the documents and skills needed to pursue engineering opportunities. Problem Statement, Planning, Ethics, and IP - Define your engineering challenge and understand the ethical and legal responsibilities that guide your work. Risk Management - Evaluate design safety and analyze risks before committing to a solution. Project Work Schedule and Budget - Plan the timeline and finances needed to complete your practicum project. Project Proposal Pitch - Write, present, and peer review your engineering project proposal. Project Execution, Checkpoints, and Final Presentation - Build, document, and present your completed engineering solution. How to Use This Textbook Each chapter aligns to one or more assignments in the course. Complete chapters in order — concepts build on each other, and later chapters assume knowledge from earlier ones. Each chapter includes a full list of concepts from the learning graph so you can track your progress. Note: Each chapter index includes a "TODO: Generate Chapter Content" placeholder. Full content will be added chapter by chapter.

docs.text: Chapter 1: Engineering Field Exploration Summary This chapter launches the course by helping students identify and research an engineering field that interests them. Students explore engineering specializations, investigate educational and licensing pathways, and compare certification and degree requirements — building the foundation for every career decision that follows in the course. Assignment Engineering Field Exploration Lab — CTE-39, CTE-40, CTE-41, CTE-42 Concepts Covered This chapter covers the following 11 concepts from the learning graph: Engineering Career Research Engineering Specializations Field of Interest Selection Degree Requirements Engineering Programs Postsecondary Education Options College Admissions Process Professional Certifications Licensure Requirements Professional Engineer (PE) License Engineer in Training (EIT) Exam Prerequisites This chapter assumes only the prerequisites listed in the course description . docs.text: Chapter 2: Engineering Foundations and Industry Examination Summary This chapter builds the conceptual foundation for the entire course by contrasting the engineering design process with the scientific method, and introducing systems thinking as a lens for understanding how engineering organizations operate. Students apply these ideas directly through the Engineering Industry Scavenger Hunt and a structured comparison activity. Assignments Engineering Industry Scavenger Hunt — CTE-23, CTE-24, CTE-25, CTE-26, CTE-27, CTE-28, CTE-29, CTE-30 Engineering Design Process vs Scientific Method Comparison — CTE-45 Concepts Covered This chapter covers the following 8 concepts from the learning graph: Scientific Method Hypothesis Testing Engineering Design Process Engineering vs Science Systems Thinking System Inputs and Outputs Feedback Loops Subsystem Interactions Prerequisites This chapter builds on concepts from: Chapter 1: Engineering Field Exploration TODO: Generate Chapter Content

docs.text: Chapter 3: Portfolio and Employment Preparation Summary This chapter prepares students to enter the engineering job market by building the core documents and skills employers look for — a professional resume, a targeted cover letter, and a complete work portfolio. It culminates in a mock interview experience that puts all of these materials to use in a realistic professional setting. Assignments Portfolio Development — CTE-43, CTE-21 Cover Letter — CTE-44, CTE-43, CTE-21 Resume — CTE-44, CTE-43, CTE-21 Professional Mock Interview Event — CTE-65 Concepts Covered This chapter covers the following 8 concepts from the learning graph: Resume Writing Cover Letter Writing Work Portfolio Portfolio Components Job Application Process Engineering Internships Interview Skills Mock Interview Practice Prerequisites This chapter builds on concepts from: Chapter 1: Engineering Field Exploration TODO: Generate Chapter Content

docs.text: Chapter 4: Problem Statement, Planning, Ethics, and IP Summary This is the launch chapter for the practicum project. Students identify a real engineering need or opportunity, define their challenge, and begin applying project management thinking to their solution. Woven throughout is an introduction to the ethical and legal responsibilities engineers carry — including professional ethics, intellectual property rights, and the obligations engineers have to the public — framed as essential context for making good design decisions. Assignment Problem Statement & Planning — CTE-49, CTE-54, CTE-57, CTE-46, CTE-47, CTE-48 Concepts Covered This chapter covers the following 21 concepts from the learning graph: Problem Identification Needs Assessment Opportunity Recognition Engineering Challenge Definition Project Management Constraints and Criteria Brainstorming Solutions Engineering Ethics Code of Ethics Professional Responsibility Public Safety in Engineering Ethical Decision Making Intellectual Property Patents Copyrights Trademarks Trade Secrets IP Protection and Enforcement IP Infringement Personal vs Professional Ethics Conflict of Interest Prerequisites This chapter builds on concepts from: Chapter 1: Engineering Field Exploration Chapter 2: Engineering Foundations and Industry Examination TODO: Generate Chapter Content

docs.text: Chapter 5: Risk Management Summary Before committing to a design direction, engineers must evaluate whether their solution is safe and identify what could go wrong. This chapter teaches students to apply safety standards to their designs, systematically identify risks, analyze their likelihood and impact, and develop strategies to reduce or eliminate them — a critical step before the project proposal is written. Assignment Risk Management — CTE-51, CTE-62 Concepts Covered This chapter covers the following 5 concepts from the learning graph: Safety Standards Design Safety Evaluation Risk Identification Risk Analysis Risk Mitigation Strategies Prerequisites This chapter builds on concepts from: Chapter 2: Engineering Foundations and Industry Examination Chapter 4: Problem Statement, Planning, Ethics, and IP TODO: Generate Chapter Content

docs.text: Chapter 6: Project Work Schedule and Budget Summary With a problem defined and risks assessed, students now plan the execution of their practicum project in detail. This chapter covers the full project planning toolkit — breaking work into manageable pieces, building a realistic schedule with industry-standard tools, and developing a budget grounded in real cost research — so students can demonstrate to stakeholders that their solution is achievable within the course timeframe. Assignments Project Work Schedule — CTE-55, CTE-59 Budget — CTE-56, CTE-58 Concepts Covered This chapter covers the following 12 concepts from the learning graph: Milestone Planning Schedule Development Scheduling Tools Gantt Chart Work Schedule Creation Work Breakdown Structure Project Management Software Resource Allocation Budget Research Methods Cost Estimation Cost-Benefit Analysis Budget Development Prerequisites This chapter builds on concepts from: Chapter 1: Engineering Field Exploration Chapter 4: Problem Statement, Planning, Ethics, and IP TODO: Generate Chapter Content

docs.text: Chapter 7: Project Proposal Pitch Summary This chapter brings together everything from the planning phase into a formal engineering proposal. Students learn how to structure and write a compelling proposal, develop the presentation and visual communication skills needed to defend it, and then experience the peer review process from both sides — as presenters and as critical reviewers providing constructive feedback. Assignment Project Proposal Pitch Presentation — CTE-52, CTE-53 Concepts Covered This chapter covers the following 8 concepts from the learning graph: Presentation Skills Visual Communication Proposal Writing Project Proposal Structure Proposal Review Criteria Constructive Feedback Proposal Defense Peer Review Process Prerequisites This chapter builds on concepts from: Chapter 4: Problem Statement, Planning, Ethics, and IP Chapter 5: Risk Management Chapter 6: Project Work Schedule and Budget TODO: Generate Chapter Content

docs.text: Learning Graph Open Learning Graph Viewer Fullscreen { .md-button .md-button--primary } Engineering Practicum IV Honors This learning graph is the foundational data structure for the Engineering Practicum IV Honors intelligent textbook. It maps 85 key concepts across three competency areas — Employability Skills, Intellectual Property, and Practicum Project Design — and defines the prerequisite relationships between them. The learning graph supports multiple learning pathways and helps students understand how concepts build upon one another throughout the course. What is a Learning Graph? A learning graph is a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) in which: Each node represents a concept students must learn Each arrow represents a learning dependency — the concept at the tail should be learned before the concept at the head Concept Categories The 85 concepts are organized into 11 categories: Foundation Concepts — Engineering design process, scientific method, and their relationship Career Exploration — Engineering fields, specializations, and postsecondary pathways Credentials and Licensing — Degrees, certifications, PE license, and EIT exam Employment Preparation — Portfolios, resumes, applications, and interviews Engineering Ethics — Professional codes, responsibility, and ethical decision making Intellectual Property — Patents, copyrights, trademarks, trade secrets, and enforcement Design Process — Problem identification through prototype development and optimization Systems Thinking — Inputs, outputs, feedback loops, and subsystem interactions Proposals and Review — Writing, structuring, defending, and peer reviewing proposals Project Management — Scheduling, budgeting, risk analysis, and project tools Communication and Documentation — Reports, modeling, stakeholder communication, and presentations Resources Course Description Assessment Concept List Concept Taxonomy Taxonomy Distribution Report Graph Quality Metrics
docs.title: Introduction

docs.title: Designing a Practicum Project (CTE-49–66)

docs.text: Concept Taxonomy: Engineering Practicum IV Honors 11 categories covering all 85 concepts across the three competency areas. Categories 1. Foundation Concepts (FOUND) Core principles that underpin the rest of the course — the engineering design process, the scientific method, and how they relate to each other. Concepts: Engineering Design Process, Scientific Method, Hypothesis Testing, Engineering vs Science 2. Career Exploration (CAREER) Researching engineering fields, specializations, educational pathways, and the credentials that lead to engineering careers. Concepts: Engineering Career Research, Engineering Specializations, Field of Interest Selection, Postsecondary Education Options, Engineering Programs, College Admissions Process, Degree Requirements, Professional Certifications, Licensure Requirements, Professional Engineer (PE) License, Engineer in Training (EIT) Exam 4. Employment Preparation (EMPLOY) Practical tools and skills for entering the engineering workforce — portfolios, resumes, applications, and interviews. Concepts: Work Portfolio, Portfolio Components, Resume Writing, Cover Letter Writing, Job Application Process, Engineering Internships, Interview Skills, Mock Interview Practice, Professional Readiness 5. Engineering Ethics (ETHICS) The ethical frameworks and responsibilities engineers hold toward society, clients, and the profession. Concepts: Engineering Ethics, Code of Ethics, Professional Responsibility, Public Safety in Engineering, Ethical Decision Making, Personal vs Professional Ethics, Conflict of Interest 6. Intellectual Property (IP) Types of intellectual property protections, how they are enforced, and the consequences of infringement. Concepts: Intellectual Property, Patents, Copyrights, Trademarks, Trade Secrets, IP Protection and Enforcement, IP Infringement 7. Design Process (DESIGN) The iterative steps of engineering design from problem identification through prototype development and optimization. Concepts: Problem Identification, Needs Assessment, Opportunity Recognition, Constraints and Criteria, Brainstorming Solutions, Prototype Development, Testing and Iteration, Design Optimization, Design Safety Evaluation, Safety Standards 8. Systems Thinking (SYST) Understanding engineering problems as interconnected systems with inputs, outputs, feedback loops, and subsystems. Concepts: Systems Thinking, System Inputs and Outputs, Feedback Loops, Subsystem Interactions 9. Proposals and Review (PROP) Writing, structuring, defending, and peer-reviewing engineering project proposals. Concepts: Proposal Writing, Project Proposal Structure, Engineering Challenge Definition, Proposal Defense, Peer Review Process, Proposal Review Criteria, Constructive Feedback 10. Project Management (PROJMGT) Planning, scheduling, budgeting, and managing risk throughout the engineering practicum project. Concepts: Project Management, Project Management Software, Work Breakdown Structure, Schedule Development, Scheduling Tools, Gantt Chart, Milestone Planning, Budget Research Methods, Cost Estimation, Cost-Benefit Analysis, Budget Development, Resource Allocation, Work Schedule Creation, Risk Identification, Risk Analysis, Risk Mitigation Strategies 11. Communication and Documentation (COMM) Documenting project progress, modeling solutions, and communicating results to stakeholders and the public. Concepts: Technical Report Writing, Documentation Standards, Report Structure, Modeling Techniques, Simulation Software, Stakeholder Identification, Stakeholder Communication, Presentation Skills, Visual Communication, Final Project Presentation

docs.text: Course Description Assessment Report Course: Engineering Practicum IV Honors Assessment Date: 2026-06-18 Assessed File: /docs/course-description.md Overall Score: 100/100 Quality Rating: Excellent — Ready for learning graph generation Detailed Scoring Breakdown Element Points Available Points Earned Title 5 5 Target Audience 5 5 Prerequisites 5 5 Main Topics Covered 10 10 Topics Excluded 5 5 Learning Outcomes Header 5 5 Remember Level 10 10 Understand Level 10 10 Apply Level 10 10 Analyze Level 10 10 Evaluate Level 10 10 Create Level 10 10 Descriptive Context 5 5 Total 100 100 Scoring Justification Title (5/5): "Engineering Practicum IV Honors" is clear, specific, and conveys course level and sequence position. Target Audience (5/5): "High school students (Honors level)" is specific and appropriately qualified. Prerequisites (5/5): Prerequisites are clearly stated — Engineering II with instructor permission, or Engineering III. Main Topics Covered (10/10): Ten well-defined topic areas covering personal qualities, team skills, professional competencies, industry examination, civic engagement, WBL, employability, ethics/IP, practicum design, and career preparation. Topics Excluded (5/5): Four clear exclusions set appropriate boundaries distinguishing high school from post-secondary engineering content. Learning Outcomes Header (5/5): Correctly uses "After this course, students will be able to:" framing. Remember (10/10): Ten specific, verb-driven outcomes using "identify," "research," and "maintain." Understand (10/10): Ten outcomes using "explain," "demonstrate," and "examine" to show conceptual comprehension. Apply (10/10): Ten outcomes using "apply," "use," "develop," "create," and "participate" tied to concrete practicum tasks. Analyze (10/10): Ten outcomes using "analyze," "compare," "examine," "reflect," and "explore" to break down complex relationships. Evaluate (10/10): Ten outcomes using "evaluate," "peer review," "demonstrate," and "manage" with clear judgment criteria. Create (10/10): Ten outcomes including the capstone practicum project, portfolio, technical report, and stakeholder presentation — strong synthesis across the sequence. Descriptive Context (5/5): Course overview effectively explains purpose, relevance, and the integrative capstone role of the course. Gap Analysis No significant gaps identified. All thirteen elements are present and meet or exceed quality thresholds. Minor observations (not point-deductions): - The "Topics NOT Covered" section could optionally be expanded with more discipline-specific exclusions (e.g., fluid dynamics, thermodynamics, structural analysis) for even clearer boundaries during concept generation. - Some Bloom's Taxonomy outcomes overlap across levels (e.g., "research" appears in both Remember and Analyze) — this is acceptable and reflects the course's integrated nature, but the learning graph generator should be aware of this when clustering concepts. Concept Generation Readiness Estimated concept potential: 200+ concepts ✓ Justification: - 10 topic areas × ~10 sub-concepts each = ~100 foundational concepts - 60 learning outcomes across 6 Bloom's levels generate additional procedural, conceptual, and metacognitive concepts - Engineering design process, project management, ethics, IP, WBL, and career pathways each contain dense concept clusters - The capstone practicum alone (systems thinking, risk analysis, scheduling, budgeting, modeling, stakeholder communication) yields 20–30 distinct concepts The course description is well-suited to support a full 200-concept learning graph. Next Steps Score ≥ 85 — Ready to proceed with learning graph generation. Recommended next skill: learning-graph-generator
docs.title: Course Description Assessment

docs.text: Taxonomy Distribution Report Course: Engineering Practicum IV Honors Total Concepts: 85 Category Name TaxonomyID Count Percentage Project Management PROJMGT 16 18.8% Design Process DESIGN 10 11.8% Communication and Documentation COMM 10 11.8% Employment Preparation EMPLOY 9 10.6% Engineering Ethics ETHICS 7 8.2% Intellectual Property IP 7 8.2% Proposals and Review PROP 7 8.2% Career Exploration CAREER 6 7.1% Credentials and Licensing CRED 5 5.9% Foundation Concepts FOUND 4 4.7% Systems Thinking SYST 4 4.7% Notes No category exceeds 30% of total concepts — distribution is healthy. Project Management (PROJMGT) is the largest category at 18.8%, reflecting the depth of the practicum project execution competencies (CTE-55 through CTE-64). Foundation Concepts (FOUND) and Systems Thinking (SYST) are intentionally small — they represent core prerequisite knowledge rather than a standalone topic area.

docs.text: Learning Graph Viewer This interactive viewer allows you to explore the learning graph for this course. Features Search : Type in the search box to find specific concepts Category Filtering : Use checkboxes to show/hide concept categories Interactive Navigation : Click and drag to explore, scroll to zoom Statistics : View real-time counts of visible nodes and edges Using the Viewer Search for Concepts : Start typing in the search box to find concepts. Click on a result to focus on that node. Filter by Category : Use the category checkboxes in the sidebar to show or hide groups of related concepts. Use "Check All" or "Uncheck All" for bulk operations. Navigate the Graph : Drag to pan around the graph Scroll to zoom in and out Click on a node to select it and highlight its connections View Statistics : The sidebar shows counts of visible nodes, edges, and foundational concepts. Graph Structure Foundational Concepts (left side): Prerequisites with no dependencies Advanced Concepts (right side): Topics that build on multiple prerequisites Edges : Arrows point from a concept to its prerequisites Launch the Viewer Open Learning Graph Viewer { .md-button .md-button--primary }
